mnemonics: Address to Data Highway: The CPU sends an address down the 'highway' (address bus), and the memory sends the data (word) back.
MAR is the 'Asker', MDR is the 'Getter': The Memory Address Register asks for the location, the Memory Data Register gets the word from that location.
keyPoints: The CPU places the desired memory address onto the address bus.
The Memory Management Unit (MMU) translates the virtual address to a physical address (if applicable).
The memory module returns the data word stored at that address, placing it onto the data bus for the CPU to read into a register (like the MDR).
commonMistakes: Assuming the data bus width is always the same as the word size; a 64-bit word might be transferred over a 32-bit data bus in two cycles.
Forgetting that the address specifies a unique byte location, and the memory controller handles fetching the entire aligned word that contains that byte.
Ignoring the role of the memory controller in coordinating the read operation, including asserting control signals like MemRead.
